content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA ·95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Tom Schroeter 
City Attorney 
City of Wasco 
254 H Street 
Bakersfield, CA 93304 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-141 
Dear Mr. Schroeter: 
August 15, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Wasco Councilmember 
Teofilo Cortez, Jr., regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the 
"Act") and Government Code Section 1090, et seq. 1 
Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code 
or common law conflicts of interest. Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering 
advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are 
complete and accurate. If this is not the case, or if the facts underlying these decisions should 
change, you should contact us for additional advice. 
In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Kem County District 
Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097. l(c)(3).) We did not receive a written 
response from either entity. (Section 1097.l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for 
purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against 
any individual other than the requester." (See Section 1097.l(c)(5).) 
QUESTION 
1. May Councilmember Cortez, Jr., assist his parents in his private capacity as a real estate 
broker in the City's negotiations to purchase his parent's real property? 
2. May he participate in the City's decision in his official capacity if he does not act as a 
broker in the transaction? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSION 
File No. A-18-141 
Page No. 2 
1. No. Councilmember Cortez has a prohibitive financial interest if he acts as a real estate 
broker in this transaction under Section 1090. Further, the City Council cannot avoid the Section 
1090 prohibition by his recusal from the City's decision-making process ifhe acts as the broker in 
this transaction. 
2. If he does not act as a broker, his family relationship alone does not rise to a prohibited 
financial interest in the City's decision under the Act or Section 1090.2 
FACTS 
The City sued manufacturers of 1,2,3-TCP over contamination of its wells several years ago, 
and settled for a sum of money. All five of the City's active wells have contamination of 1,2,3 TCP. 
The City is now drilling new wells to address that issue and purchasing property for that purpose. 
Councilmember Cortez is a licensed real estate broker with a firm in the city of Wasco. He 
has an ownership interest of more than 10 percent in the brokerage business. Recently, the City 
identified property owned by the Councilmember's parents, Mr. and Mrs. Cortez, Sr., as necessary 
for the construction of a water well to address the water contamination problem. Councilmember 
Cortez has no interest in the property. He does not have any financial relationship with his parents; 
nor have his parents been a source of income to him in the past twelve months. He anticipates that 
he will be assisting his parents in his capacity as a real estate broker in any negotiations that they 
have with the City. He anticipates that he will not receive any compensation for this assistance. 
The City is presently negotiating the purchase of two properties and will negotiate the 
.purchase of the property owned by the Councilmember's parents if the Councilmember does not 
have a conflict. The City will be negotiating with his parents to purchase the property, or a portion 
of it. If the negotiations are successful, the City will enter into a purchase and sale agreement with 
his parents, open an escrow, and execute escrow documents which would result in recording a deed 
transferring ownership of the property from his parents to the City. The City Council will approve 
all aspects of the purchase. 
The City is negotiating for the purchase of another property as a back-up for the Cortez 
property if a conflict prevents the purchase of his parents' property. The City may need additional 
property in the future for other contaminants, but for 1,2,3-TCP that it must treat now, the 
immediate need is t.o acquire the three properties. 
ANALYSIS 
Question 1. May Councilmember Cortez, Jr., assist his parents in his private capacity as a 
real estate broker in the City's purchase of real property owned by his parents? 
2 This analysis does not extend to applicable prohibitions on participation under the common-law doctrine 
against conflicts of interest. 


Section 1090 
File No. A-18-141 
Page No. 3 
Section 1090 provides that public officials "shall not be financially interested in any contract 
made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members." The 
prohibition applies regardless of whether the terms are fair and equitable to all parties.3 Section 
1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent 
public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best 
interests of their agencies.4 A conflicted official may not avoid the application of Section 1090 by 
abstaining from participating in the body's decision-making process on the contract.5 
Although Section 1090 does not specifically define the term "financial interest," case law 
and Attorney General opinions state that prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as 
direct, and may involve financial losses, or the possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of 
pecuniary gain.6 Similarly, "financial interest" is liberally interpreted and courts look to whether the 
officer had "the opportunity to, and did, influence execution directly or indirectly to promote his 
personal interests. "7 
The Legislature has expressly defined a real estate broker's interest in a contract as 
"remote" where the real estate broker has an ownership interest of 10 percent or more in the real 
estate firm, and the broker has not received and will not receive remuneration, consideration or a 
commission as a result of the contract. (Section 1091 (b )( 6). )8 This remote interest has been 
interpreted to apply in situations where the official's representation of the client is in other, 
unrelated matters, and not in the particular contract with the agency.9 
A recent Attorney General Opinion addressed the application of Section 1091 (b )( 6), to a 
city councilmember who acted as an attorney for a contracting party with interests adverse to the 
3 Thomson v. Call ( 1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646-649. 
4 Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569. 
5 Fraser-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. County of Del Norte (1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201, 211-212. 
6 People v. Vallerga (1977) 67 Cal.App.3d 847, 867, fn. 5; Terry v. Bender (1956) 143 Cal.App.2d 198, 207-
208; 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36-38 (2002); 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 161-162 (2001). 
7 People v. Sobel (1974) 40 Cal.App.3d 1046, 1052. 
8 Section 109l(b)(6) states that an officer has only a remote interest in the contract when the interest is: 
(6) That ofan attorney of the contracting party or that ofan owner, officer, employee, or 
agent of a firm that renders, or has rendered, service to the contracting party in the 
capacity of stockbroker, insurance agent, insurance broker, real estate agent, or real 
estate broker, if these individuals have not received and will not receive remuneration, 
consideration, or a commission as a result of the contract and if these individuals have 
an ownership interest of IO percent or more in the law practice or firm, stock brokerage 
firm, insurance firm, or real estate firm. 
9 78 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 230,237 (1995). [remote interest exception in Section 109l(b)(6) applied to city 
councilmember whose spouse's law firm represented developer but not in regard to the development contracts at issue.] 


File No. A-18-141 
Page No. 4 
city. 10 The Attorney General found that this remote exception does not apply where the 
representation involves the contract at issue. 11 The Attorney General stated that this narrow 
interpretation of the exception is necessary to avoid undermining the purpose of Section 1090 in 
ensuring that public officials discharge their fiduciary duties with undivided allegiance. As to the 
financial interest, the Attorney General stated that the "outcome would not vary merely because a 
representation was without compensation," because "pro bono representation may still produce 
economic gains, goodwill, or prestige" for an official's business."12 
Similarly, Councilmember Cortez's assistance to his parents in the contract negotiations 
with the City does not fall under the remote exception, and he will have a prohibited financial 
interest in the contract under Section 1090. His representation is not on an unrelated matter, but on 
the sale negotiations with the City. As to his financial interest in the matter, we note that these 
circumstances involve more than a mere pro bono review of a simple purchase and sale agreement. 
The Councilmember would act as the authorized broker for his clients, Mr. and Mrs. Cortez, in 
active negotiations with the City over the price and terms of the property's sale. The City is 
presently negotiating on two other parcels, and has an alternate to the Cortez property to consider. 
Thus, his pro bono work may result in costs to his brokerage business through his time dedicated to 
these negotiations. It may also bring indirect economic gain to his brokerage business by enhancing 
the prestige, good will and publicity of the business in the community. 
If he chooses to assist his parents in negotiations with the City, the City Council cannot 
avoid the Section 1090 prohibition by his recusal from the decision-making process. In the case of a 
financially-interested board member, the official generally cannot avoid the conflict by 
disqualification; rather he or she must resign from office or eliminate the private interest to avoid 
the proscription of Section 1090. (See City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191; 
Finnegan v. Schrader (2001) 91 Cal.App.4th 572.) Due to this prohibition, no further analysis is 
necessary under the Act as to whether the Councilmember may act as the real estate broker for his 
parents in this transaction. 
Question 2. May Councilmember Cortez, Jr., participate in the City's decision to purchase 
his parent's property in his official capacity if he does not act as a broker in the transaction? 
Section 1090 
Past Attorney General Opinions have found that absent a financial relationship between 
adult relatives (such as a legal obligation to support an adult child, a parent's incapacity, or a shared 
ownership interest) the officer does not have a "financial interest" under Section 1090 when the 
10 101 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 1 (2018). [a city councilmember who is also an attorney for a contracting party 
whose interests are adverse to the city has a financial interest under Section 1090 and may not advocate on behalf of a 
client's interests regardless if the representation is on a pro bono basis.] 
II Id., fu. 76. 
12/d.,fn. 82, citing 86 Ops. Cal.Atty.Gen 138 (2003) [section 1090 would be violated if city entered retainer 
contract with law firm of which council member is a partner, even if the law firm would receive no fees or 
compensation. The law firm may face possible economic losses in the representation in staffing and litigation expenses; 
and the firm might reap prestige, publicity and good will that could bring indirect economic gain to the law firm.] 


contract involves an official's adult relative. 13 
File No. A-18-141 
Page No. 5 
Under the facts provided, Councilmember Cortez does not have a financial relationship with 
his parents and he has no financial interest his parent's real property. Therefore, under Section 
1090, the Councilmember does not have a financial interest in the real estate transaction related to 
his family relationship that would prohibit his participation in the City's decision. 
The Act 
A separate and distinct analysis is necessary to determine whether he has a prohibited 
financial interest in the City's decision due to his family relationship under the Act. Section 87100 
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her 
official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
Under Section 87103, a public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision ifit is 
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the 
public official's interests. These interests may include: 
• 
Business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more 
(Section 87103(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).) 
• 
Real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. 
(Section 87103(b).) 
• 
Source of income aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received 
by, the official within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) 
• 
Personal finances, including those of the official's immediate family. (Section 87103.) 
Where he seeks to act only in his official capacity, the Councilmember does not have a 
financial interest implicated by this decision. Acting in his official capacity only, his brokerage 
business would not be involved. He does not have an interest in the real property at issue. His 
parents have not been a source of income to him within 12 months of the decision. And, while he 
has a financial interest in the finances of his "immediate family," this term is defined to include a 
spouse or dependent children. 14 He does not have a financial interest in his parents' finances under 
the Act. 
· 
While his participation in the decision in his role as a member of the City Council is not 
prohibited under the Act, or Section 1090, we note that a family relationship may require recusal 
13 92 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 19, (2009) [official did not have a financial interest under Section 1090 in agency's 
loan to son's solely-held corporation despite parent-adult child relationship and shared apartment rental where there 
were no circumstances showing a legal duty for either party to support the other]; and citing 28 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 168, 
169 (1956) [family relationship between county supervisor and his adult brother did not result in a violation of Section 
I 090 where the brother sold automobiles to the county but there was not a proprietary interest or entitlement for 
contribution or support between the adult brothers] 
14 "Immediate family" is defined as a spouse or dependent children. (Section 82029.) 


File No. A-18-141 
Page No. 6 
under the common-law doctrine against conflicts of interest, a doctrine that is beyond the scope of 
this analysis. 15 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
LKH:j'gl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Acting Gene~al Counsel 
By: 
L. Karen Harrison 
Counsel, Legal Division 
15 See 92 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 19, supra, for a discussion of the common-law doctrine against conflicts of 
interest where there may be a temptation to act for personal or private reasons rather than in the public interest.

broker in the transaction?

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. May Councilmember Cortez, Jr., assist his parents in his private capacity as a real estate 
broker in the City's negotiations to purchase his parent's real property? 
2. May he participate in the City's decision in his official capacity if he does not act as a 
broker in the transaction?

CONCLUSION: 1. No. Councilmember Cortez has a prohibitive financial interest if he acts as a real estate 
broker in this transaction under Section 1090. Further, the City Council cannot avoid the Section 
1090 prohibition by his recusal from the City's decision-making process ifhe acts as the broker in 
this transaction. 
2. If he does not act as a broker, his family relationship alone does not rise to a prohibited 
financial interest in the City's decision under the Act or Section 1090.2
